text: I need one decision from you by Friday.
Jargon
Analogy
Justify
Benefit
Cost-effective
Maintain
Legacy
Complexity
We are replacing the database, which means the system does not stop on Mondays.
The new system is cheaper to maintain than the old one.
The legacy database is like a car from 2010.
In plain terms, we lose four hours every week.
Diogo, sixty seconds. Why are we spending four weeks on a system that already works?
In plain terms: we spend four hours every week keeping it alive. That is a whole morning, every week.
Four hours is not four weeks. Justify the four weeks.
The legacy database is like a car from 2010. Every repair is cheap, so we keep repairing. Six times a year.
And the new one? Do not give me jargon.
It is cheaper to maintain, and safer, which means the client's data is not in a system that nobody supports.
The client will ask why they pay for four weeks of nothing.
They do not pay for nothing. They pay four hours a week today, forever. The benefit starts in July.
Now that I can defend. Send me that in one slide, with the analogy.
We are replacing it, which means the system does not stop.
The new one is cheaper to maintain, and safer.
If we do nothing, we pay the same four hours forever.

Diogo, you are on the agenda for the steering committee at three. You have sixty seconds, and I want to warn you about this room. The CFO is there. She does not know what a container is, and she does not want to know. Last quarter an architect used the word Kubernetes twice and she stopped listening after the first one. So do not open with the technology. Open with money or time. Tell her what we lose today, and what we get back. If you need to explain how it works, give her an analogy, something from real life. And whatever you do, do not say the word migration. Say what changes for the client.

Thank you. In plain terms: every week, my team spends four hours keeping a fifteen-year-old system alive. Four hours that the client pays for and does not see. We are replacing that system, which means those four hours go back to client work, every week, from July. Here is an analogy. The legacy database is like a car from 2010. Every repair is cheap, so we keep repairing it -- six times a year. At some point you are not saving money, you are just spending it slowly. The new system is cheaper to maintain, and it is safer, because the old one is out of support and it stores client data. If we do nothing, we pay the same four hours a week, forever. I need four weeks and no new features in that time. After that, the team is faster, and the risk is lower.

Sophia gives Diogo sixty seconds and asks why the project needs four weeks. Diogo opens with time: four hours every week keeping the old system alive. She asks him to justify the four weeks, and he gives the analogy of the car from 2010. He explains the benefit: cheaper to maintain, and safer for the client's data. Finally, Sophia asks for the whole answer in one slide.

If we do nothing, we pay the same four hours forever.
